**Buttermilk** or **cultured buttermilk** is a tangy liquid dairy product.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Buttermilk originally referred to the liquid left behind after churning butter[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)—this was often tangy due to fermentation of the cream prior to churning,[[4]](#cite_note-4) as well as low in fat due to the separation of the butter. Today, commercial cultured buttermilk is made separately from the butter churning process by simply inoculating low-fat milk with lactic acid bacteria.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

Because most of the fat is removed with the butter, buttermilk is not very rich. It is sour and thicker than milk due to the acid's effect on the dairy.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) Buttermilk is sometimes available in a dry powdered form.

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Buttermilk should be stored in the fridge. It has a relatively long shelf life, but it will go bad.

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Buttermilk is quite popular as a refreshment in India, where it is made into a drink known as lassi"). When used in baked goods, buttermilk tenderizes them in addition to contributing flavor and leavening (if combined with baking soda).[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:2-5)

If you don't have buttermilk on hand, there are a couple acceptable substitutes. Plain kefir is very similar and can generally be substituted exactly. Sour milk can be made by adding a little vinegar or lemon/lime juice to milk, and this will contribute acidity.[[3]](#cite_note-:1-3) It will, however, have a different flavor profile and thickness. To avoid this, you can also dilute yogurt or sour cream with milk until the correct consistency.
